lectures.title: Lecture 6: GI Physiology I — Overview & Upper GI

questions.q: Which of the following is NOT a function of the digestive system?
questions.explain: The digestive system ingests, digests, and absorbs nutrients. Synthesis of enzymes occurs in the pancreas and salivary glands, but the system itself does not synthesize—it secretes pre-made enzymes.

questions.q: Which type of digestion is primarily responsible for reducing the number of molecules?
questions.explain: Chemical (enzymatic) digestion breaks large molecules into smaller ones. Mechanical digestion prepares food for enzyme action but does not reduce the number of molecules.

questions.q: Which nutrient type is primarily broken down into monosaccharides in the digestive system?
questions.explain: Carbohydrates are hydrolyzed into monosaccharides (glucose, fructose, galactose) by amylase and disaccharidases.

questions.q: What is the primary end product of protein digestion?
questions.explain: Proteases break proteins into amino acids and small peptides for absorption. Complete digestion yields amino acids; partial digestion yields peptides.

questions.q: The four main layers of the GI tract wall, from innermost to outermost, are:
questions.explain: From innermost to outermost: mucosa (secretion/absorption), submucosa (connective tissue and vessels), muscularis (smooth muscle), serosa (outer covering).

questions.q: Which structure of the mouth is primarily responsible for manipulating the food bolus during chewing?
questions.explain: Teeth mechanically break food; the tongue manipulates and moves the bolus, preparing it for swallowing.

questions.q: Salivary amylase begins starch digestion, but pancreatic amylase is responsible for most starch hydrolysis. Why is this the case?
options: Salivary amylase is denatured in the stomach
Pancreatic amylase is much more efficient
Salivary amylase acts for only a short time in the mouth and early esophagus
Pancreatic amylase is activated by gastric juice
questions.explain: Salivary amylase functions only briefly—about 5–10 minutes in the mouth and early esophagus before being inactivated by gastric acid. Pancreatic amylase in the small intestine (pH 7–8, optimal for amylase) handles the majority of starch digestion.

questions.q: Which of the following is the CORRECT match between a GI secretion and its primary source?
questions.explain: Bile is from the liver, pepsinogen from the stomach's chief cells, pancreatic amylase from the pancreas, and mucus is secreted by salivary glands and stomach mucous cells.

questions.q: The total daily GI secretion is 7–10 liters, yet fecal water loss is only ~100 mL/day. Which of the following explains this?
options: Most water is lost through urine
The small intestine and colon reabsorb ~99% of secreted water
Water is not actually secreted in large amounts
Most water evaporates in the stomach
questions.explain: Despite large secretion, the small intestine and colon reabsorb most water passively following active ion absorption, leaving only ~100 mL in feces.

questions.q: Which of the following best describes peristalsis?
options: Rhythmic local constrictions that mix food without net forward movement
Coordinated waves of muscular contraction that propel food forward
Churning motion confined to the stomach
Random muscular contractions of the esophagus
questions.explain: Peristalsis is a coordinated wave propelling food forward. Segmentation (rhythmic local constrictions) mixes without net forward movement—they are distinct.

questions.q: Which region of the small intestine is the shortest?
questions.explain: The duodenum is ~26 cm (5% of small intestine length); jejunum is ~2.5 m (40%); ileum is ~3.5 m (55%).

questions.q: The gross surface area of the small intestine is approximately 0.33 m². Which adaptations amplify this to ~200 m²?
options: Circular folds, villi, and microvilli together
questions.explain: All three levels of structure (circular folds, villi, microvilli) contribute to a ~600-fold amplification of surface area.

questions.q: Which of the following is an example of secondary active transport (cotransport) in the small intestine?
options: Glucose via GLUT2 at the basolateral membrane
Glucose via SGLT1 (Na+ glucose symporter) at the apical membrane
Fructose via GLUT5 at the apical membrane
questions.explain: SGLT1 is a Na+-coupled glucose symporter (secondary active transport, using the Na+ gradient created by the Na+/K+ ATPase). GLUT2 and GLUT5 are facilitated diffusion; aquaporins are passive.

questions.q: Amino acids are absorbed primarily via:
options: Simple diffusion down their concentration gradient
questions.explain: Amino acids cross the apical membrane via active transport mechanisms coupled to Na+ entry, similar to glucose absorption.

questions.q: In fat absorption, why is lipid re-esterification in the epithelial cell important?
options: To store energy for the epithelial cell
To increase the lipid's solubility for transport into chylomicrons and lacteals
questions.explain: Pancreatic lipase breaks triglycerides down to monoglycerides and fatty acids (hydrophilic). Re-esterification in the epithelial cell converts them back to triglycerides (hydrophobic), allowing packaging into chylomicrons for lymphatic transport.

questions.q: Chylomicrons enter the bloodstream via:
options: Blood capillaries in the intestinal villi
questions.explain: Fats packaged in chylomicrons enter lacteals (lymph), not blood capillaries initially—they bypass first-pass hepatic metabolism.

questions.q: Which of the following is NOT a function of the colon?
options: Fermentation of undigested carbohydrates by bacteria
Production of short-chain fatty acids for energy
Digestion of proteins into amino acids
questions.explain: The colon absorbs water and electrolytes, hosts bacterial fermentation, and bacteria produce SCFAs. Protein digestion is complete in the small intestine.

questions.q: The bacterial flora of the colon produce short-chain fatty acids (SCFAs) from:
questions.explain: Bacterial fermentation of undigested carbohydrates produces SCFAs (acetate, butyrate, propionate), which are absorbed as an energy source.

questions.q: Which of the following is a reason the pancreatic enzymes are secreted as inactive zymogens?
options: To prevent self-digestion of the pancreatic tissue
To allow for better absorption in the small intestine
To reduce the osmotic load in the pancreatic ducts
questions.explain: Zymogens (inactive enzyme precursors) prevent the pancreas from digesting itself. They are activated in the duodenum by enterokinase and other proteases.

questions.q: Which segment of the small intestine is specialized for vitamin B12 absorption?
questions.explain: The ileum has specific receptors for the intrinsic factor–B12 complex, enabling B12 absorption in this distal region.

questions.q: The major sites of nutrient absorption in the small intestine are:
options: Distributed equally across all three segments
questions.explain: The duodenum is primarily for neutralization and enzyme entry. The jejunum and ileum are the major absorption sites.

questions.q: Which of the following correctly describes the movement of ions and water in the colon?
options: Water is actively transported; ions follow passively
Ions are actively reabsorbed; water follows passively via osmosis
Both move passively via simple diffusion
questions.explain: Active Na+ and Cl- reabsorption in the colon creates an osmotic gradient, driving passive water reabsorption.

questions.q: Bile salts are essential for fat absorption because they:
options: Hydrolyze triglycerides into fatty acids and glycerol
questions.explain: Bile salts emulsify fats into small droplets and form micelles with lipid digestion products, facilitating their approach to the brush border for absorption.

questions.q: A patient with impaired bile production would have difficulty absorbing:
options: Fat-soluble vitamins (A, D, E, K) and lipids
questions.explain: Bile is essential for fat and fat-soluble vitamin absorption. Carbohydrates, amino acids, and water-soluble vitamins are unaffected by bile deficiency.

questions.q: Which of the following is a correct sequence of carbohydrate digestion and absorption?
options: Disaccharides → monosaccharides → absorbed via SGLT1 and GLUT5 → GLUT2 at basolateral side
Polysaccharides → amylase breaks to oligosaccharides → disaccharidases break to monosaccharides → absorption
Starches → glucose → absorbed in stomach → enters blood
Fructose → glucose → absorbed via SGLT1 → GLUT2
questions.explain: Amylase breaks polysaccharides to oligosaccharides; brush-border disaccharidases complete hydrolysis to monosaccharides for absorption.

questions.q: Exam-style: In a patient with pancreatic insufficiency (low enzyme secretion), which nutrient would be most severely malabsorbed?
questions.explain: Although all are affected, fats are most severely affected because pancreatic lipase is the primary fat-digesting enzyme, and bile salts alone cannot accomplish hydrolysis.

questions.q: Which of the following is a major difference between the duodenum and the jejunum?
options: The duodenum is longer than the jejunum
The duodenum receives pancreatic and biliary secretions; the jejunum does not
The jejunum is where most nutrient absorption occurs
The duodenum has more villi than the jejunum
questions.explain: While the duodenum receives and processes secretions from pancreas and liver, the jejunum and ileum are the major sites of nutrient absorption. The duodenum is actually shorter.

questions.q: Which of the following best describes the relationship between digestion and absorption?
options: Digestion must occur before absorption can happen
questions.explain: Absorption requires molecules to be reduced to absorbable size via digestion first.

questions.q: The esophagus contributes to digestion by:
options: Continuing starch digestion with salivary amylase
Serving as a conduit for food transport
questions.explain: The esophagus has no significant secretion, digestion, or absorption—its role is purely mechanical transport of food.

questions.q: Which ion is reabsorbed in the large intestine, creating the osmotic gradient that draws water with it?
questions.explain: Active reabsorption of Na+ (and Cl−) in the colon creates an osmotic gradient that drives passive water reabsorption.

lectures.title: Lecture 7: GI Physiology II — Digestion & Pancreas/Liver

questions.q: Which of the following accurately describes the composition of pancreatic juice?
questions.explain: Pancreatic juice contains water, electrolytes (bicarbonate, sodium, potassium, chloride), and digestive enzymes (trypsin, lipase, amylase). It does not contain mucus or bile.

questions.q: What is the primary role of pancreatic bicarbonate in the small intestine?
options: Neutralize gastric acid and raise intestinal pH
questions.explain: Pancreatic bicarbonate neutralizes acidic chyme from the stomach, raising small intestinal pH to the optimal range (7-8) for pancreatic enzyme activity.

questions.q: Trypsin is secreted by the pancreas as trypsinogen. Why is this inactive precursor form necessary?
options: To prevent autolysis of the pancreas itself
questions.explain: Trypsinogen is the inactive form. If active trypsin were secreted, it would digest the pancreas's own proteins. Enterokinase in the small intestine converts trypsinogen to active trypsin.

questions.q: Which hormone stimulates pancreatic bicarbonate secretion in response to acidic chyme entering the small intestine?
questions.explain: Secretin is released by S cells in the duodenum when pH drops below 4.5. It directly stimulates the pancreas to secrete bicarbonate-rich juice.

questions.q: Which of the following pancreatic enzymes is responsible for breaking down proteins into amino acids and small peptides?
questions.explain: Trypsin and chymotrypsin are endopeptidases that cleave peptide bonds within proteins. Carboxypeptidases then trim amino acids from the ends.

questions.q: Why does pancreatic amylase require pancreatic bicarbonate to function optimally in the small intestine?
options: The enzyme is inactive below pH 7
Gastric acid denatures pancreatic amylase in the stomach
questions.explain: Pancreatic amylase has an optimal pH of 7-8 and is inactivated by the acidic environment of the stomach. Pancreatic bicarbonate neutralizes the acid, allowing the enzyme to function.

questions.q: Pancreatic lipase is the primary enzyme for fat digestion. Under which pH condition does it work most effectively?
questions.explain: Pancreatic lipase functions optimally at pH 7-8 (neutral to slightly alkaline), which is the environment of the small intestine after pancreatic bicarbonate has neutralized gastric acid.

questions.q: Which hormone stimulates the pancreas to secrete digestive enzymes in response to the presence of food (especially fats and proteins) in the small intestine?
questions.explain: CCK is released by I cells in the duodenum and jejunum in response to fats and amino acids. It stimulates the pancreas to secrete enzyme-rich juice.

questions.q: What is the functional role of colipase in lipid digestion?
options: Break down triglycerides into glycerol and fatty acids
Emulsify fats for easier enzyme access
Facilitate pancreatic lipase attachment to fat droplets
questions.explain: Colipase is a cofactor for pancreatic lipase. It binds to lipase and helps anchor the enzyme to fat droplets, increasing the enzyme's efficiency.

questions.q: Approximately how much pancreatic juice is secreted per day in response to meals?
questions.explain: The pancreas secretes approximately 2 L of juice per day, containing both bicarbonate (for pH control) and digestive enzymes.

questions.q: Which of the following is NOT a function of the liver in digestion?
options: Regulate blood glucose and amino acid levels
questions.explain: The liver produces bile (not enzymes), regulates metabolism, stores vitamins, and has many other functions. Digestive enzymes are produced by the pancreas and salivary glands, not the liver.

questions.q: What is the primary component of bile that aids in fat digestion?
questions.explain: Bile salts are amphipathic molecules that emulsify fats, breaking them into smaller droplets to increase the surface area available for pancreatic lipase action.

questions.q: Bile is continuously produced by the liver and stored in the gallbladder. What is the trigger for gallbladder contraction and bile release?
options: Presence of carbohydrates in the mouth
Presence of fats and amino acids in the duodenum
questions.explain: Cholecystokinin (CCK) is released in response to fats and proteins (amino acids) in the duodenum, causing the gallbladder to contract and release stored bile into the small intestine.

questions.q: Under the microscope, how would you distinguish bile salts from other components of bile based on their chemical properties?
options: They are the only hydrophobic component
They are amphipathic (containing both hydrophobic and hydrophilic regions)
They form crystals at room temperature
questions.explain: Bile salts are amphipathic: the hydrophobic side interacts with fat, while the hydrophilic side faces the aqueous environment. This structure makes them ideal emulsifiers.

questions.q: Approximately how much bile is produced by the liver per day?
questions.explain: The liver produces 0.6-1.2 L of bile per day. This bile is either released directly into the duodenum or stored in the gallbladder for later release.

questions.q: What happens to bile salts after they have completed their function in fat emulsification?
options: They are degraded by intestinal bacteria
They are reabsorbed in the terminal ileum and returned to the liver via the portal blood
questions.explain: Bile salts are efficiently recycled through enterohepatic circulation: ~95% are reabsorbed in the terminal ileum and returned to the liver, where they are re-secreted. This conserves bile salts.

questions.q: Which component of bile serves no direct digestive role but is a breakdown product of hemoglobin metabolism?
questions.explain: Bile pigments (bilirubin, biliverdin) are waste products from the breakdown of old red blood cells. They give bile its color but don't participate in fat digestion.

questions.q: Why is cholesterol present in bile even though it doesn't directly aid in digestion?
options: It emulsifies fats alongside bile salts
It is a waste product eliminated through bile
questions.explain: Cholesterol in bile is a waste product and a means of excretion. The liver uses bile to eliminate cholesterol from the body, which is why cholesterol levels are one measure of liver health.

questions.q: If the gallbladder is removed, how would fat digestion be affected?
options: Fat digestion would be completely blocked
Fat digestion would proceed normally because the liver still produces bile
Fat digestion would be slower and less efficient due to lack of stored, concentrated bile
Fat digestion would increase because bile would be released continuously
questions.explain: Without a gallbladder, bile cannot be stored and concentrated. Dilute bile from the liver drips continuously into the intestine, so fat digestion is slower and less efficient, especially after fatty meals.

questions.q: Which of the following correctly pairs a pancreatic enzyme with its substrate?
questions.explain: Nuclease breaks down nucleic acids (DNA/RNA). Trypsin breaks peptide bonds in proteins, lipase breaks triglycerides, and amylase breaks starch.

questions.q: What is an exam trap regarding pancreatic enzyme secretion timing?
options: Enzymes are secreted only during meals
Enzymes are secreted in response to CCK, not Secretin
Pancreatic amylase is the only enzyme secreted in response to starch
The pancreas produces ALL enzymes as inactive zymogens to prevent self-digestion
questions.explain: Exam trap: Most pancreatic proteases are secreted as inactive zymogens, but pancreatic amylase and lipase are secreted in their active forms. This is a common test question.

questions.q: Why must bile salts be amphipathic for effective fat emulsification?
options: Amphipathic molecules are larger and thus more effective
They can interact with both the fat (hydrophobic) and water (hydrophilic) environments
Amphipathic structure increases their pH-buffering capacity
They allow the liver to produce less bile
questions.explain: Amphipathic structure is essential: the hydrophobic region inserts into fat droplets while the hydrophilic region points toward the aqueous solution, stabilizing emulsified droplets.

questions.q: A 45-year-old patient has a gallstone blocking the common bile duct. Which of the following would MOST LIKELY occur?
options: Excess bile pigments in the blood, leading to jaundice
questions.explain: Blockage prevents bile (including pigments) from reaching the intestine, so it backs up into the blood → jaundice. Fat digestion is impaired, not carbohydrate digestion.

questions.q: Pancreatic insufficiency (loss of pancreatic function) would result in impaired digestion of which nutrients?
questions.explain: The pancreas secretes amylase (carbs), proteases (proteins), and lipase (fats). Loss of pancreatic function impairs digestion of all three macronutrients.

questions.q: Which of the following accurately describes the difference between salivary and pancreatic amylase in terms of their sites of action?
options: Salivary amylase acts in the mouth; pancreatic amylase acts in the small intestine
Both act in the small intestine
Pancreatic amylase acts in the mouth; salivary amylase acts in the small intestine
questions.explain: Salivary amylase works briefly in the mouth and early esophagus before being denatured by gastric acid. Pancreatic amylase functions in the small intestine at optimal pH (7-8).

questions.q: An exam trap about pancreatic secretion: Which statement is INCORRECT?
options: The pancreas secretes both aqueous and enzymatic components
Pancreatic juice is always acidic to match the stomach environment
questions.explain: Exam trap: Pancreatic juice is ALKALINE (due to bicarbonate), not acidic. Its role is to neutralize stomach acid, not match it.

questions.q: Why is the enterohepatic circulation of bile salts considered an efficient system?
options: It reduces the workload of the liver
It conserves ~95% of bile salts, reducing the need for new synthesis
It prevents bacteria from accessing bile salts
questions.explain: The body recycles bile salts 6-8 times daily. This efficient recycling means the liver only needs to synthesize ~0.5g of new bile salts daily to replace losses, conserving energy and resources.

questions.q: Under normal conditions, which TWO hormones regulate pancreatic secretion in response to a meal?
questions.explain: Secretin (in response to acid) stimulates bicarbonate secretion; CCK (in response to fats/proteins) stimulates enzyme secretion. Both hormones work together for complete pancreatic response.

questions.q: Which of the following correctly describes the relationship between pancreatic enzyme secretion and meal composition?
options: Enzymes are secreted equally in response to all meals
Fat and protein meals trigger more enzyme secretion than carbohydrate meals
Carbohydrate meals trigger more enzyme secretion than fat meals
Enzyme secretion is independent of meal composition
questions.explain: CCK (triggered by fats/proteins) stimulates greater pancreatic enzyme secretion. Secretin (triggered by acid) stimulates bicarbonate. Fat-rich meals produce the strongest pancreatic response.

questions.q: What is the significance of the hepatic portal circulation in relation to bile salt recycling?
options: It allows bile salts to reach the intestine
It returns reabsorbed bile salts from the terminal ileum back to the liver for re-secretion
It prevents bile salts from reaching the bloodstream
It dilutes bile salts to reduce their potency
questions.explain: The portal blood carries reabsorbed bile salts directly from the terminal ileum back to the liver, where they are re-secreted into bile. This efficient recycling is the basis of enterohepatic circulation.

lectures.title: Lecture 8: GI Physiology III — Absorption — Small & Large Intestine

questions.q: Which of the following is the primary structural adaptation of the small intestine that increases surface area for absorption?
questions.explain: Villi (finger-like projections) and microvilli (brush border on epithelial cells) dramatically increase surface area. A 3-4 meter small intestine has a surface area equivalent to a tennis court.

questions.q: Approximately how many-fold does the brush border (microvilli) increase the absorptive surface area compared to a flat epithelium?
questions.explain: Villi increase surface area 10-fold; microvilli increase it another 20-40 fold; total combined increase is approximately 300-600 fold compared to a flat epithelium.

questions.q: Which nutrient is primarily absorbed in the duodenum and jejunum of the small intestine?
questions.explain: Glucose and amino acids (products of carbohydrate and protein digestion) are actively absorbed in the duodenum and jejunum. Other nutrients are absorbed further along.

questions.q: What is the primary role of the intestinal epithelial cells in absorption?
options: Transport nutrients from the lumen into the bloodstream
questions.explain: Enterocytes (epithelial cells) have apical membranes (facing the lumen) with transporters and basolateral membranes (facing the blood) to actively move absorbed nutrients across.

questions.q: Which absorption mechanism is used for glucose in the small intestine?
options: Active transport (SGLT1) coupled with sodium ions
questions.explain: Glucose is absorbed via SGLT1 (sodium-glucose transporter), which couples glucose uptake to sodium ion movement down its electrochemical gradient. This is active transport.

questions.q: How are long-chain fatty acids (products of fat digestion) absorbed across the intestinal epithelium?
options: By simple diffusion into the epithelial cell
By formation of micelles with bile salts, then passive diffusion into cells
questions.explain: Bile salts form micelles around fatty acids and monoglycerides. These micelles approach the brush border, and fatty acids passively diffuse into enterocytes.

questions.q: What happens to absorbed fatty acids inside the intestinal epithelial cell?
options: They are immediately released into the bloodstream
They are re-esterified into triglycerides and packaged into chylomicrons
questions.explain: Enterocytes re-synthesize triglycerides from absorbed fatty acids and monoglycerides, package them into chylomicrons with fat-soluble vitamins and cholesterol, then release them into the lymphatic system.

questions.q: Which of the following vitamins requires bile salts for absorption?
questions.explain: Fat-soluble vitamins (A, D, E, K) are incorporated into micelles along with fatty acids. Without bile salts, their absorption is severely impaired.

questions.q: Where in the small intestine is vitamin B12 (cobalamin) primarily absorbed?
questions.explain: Vitamin B12 is specifically absorbed in the terminal ileum through a receptor-mediated process involving intrinsic factor (from the stomach). This is a unique, specialized absorption site.

questions.q: Which of the following mechanisms explains why the large intestine absorbs water so efficiently?
options: The large intestine has more villi than the small intestine
Water is actively pumped from the lumen into the blood
Osmotic gradients created by sodium and chloride absorption drive water reabsorption
The large intestine secretes additional water to stimulate absorption
questions.explain: The large intestine absorbs sodium and chloride via active transport and exchange mechanisms. This creates an osmotic gradient that drives water reabsorption via aquaporins (water channels).

questions.q: Approximately how much water is absorbed by the large intestine per day?
questions.explain: The large intestine absorbs approximately 5-6 liters of water daily. Without this absorption, severe diarrhea would result. This makes the large intestine critical for water homeostasis.

questions.q: What is the primary role of colonic bacteria in the large intestine?
options: Produce short-chain fatty acids (SCFAs) by fermenting undigested carbohydrates
questions.explain: Colonic bacteria ferment undigestible carbohydrates (fiber) to produce SCFAs (butyrate, propionate, acetate), which nourish colonocytes. Bacteria also synthesize vitamin K and some B vitamins.

questions.q: Which electrolytes are actively reabsorbed by the large intestine?
questions.explain: The large intestine actively absorbs sodium via epithelial sodium channels (ENaC) and the sodium-chloride cotransporter. Potassium reabsorption also occurs. This maintains electrolyte balance.

questions.q: A patient with diarrhea has dramatically increased stool frequency and water loss. Which absorption defect is occurring?
options: Failure to absorb glucose in the small intestine
Reduced water reabsorption in the large intestine due to osmotic or secretory factors
questions.explain: Diarrhea indicates impaired water reabsorption (osmotic diarrhea: unabsorbed solutes retain water; secretory: excess electrolyte secretion). Normal large intestine absorption is critical for stool consistency.

questions.q: How do amino acids and dipeptides enter intestinal epithelial cells after protein digestion?
options: Active transport via specific amino acid transporters and peptide transporters (PepT1)
questions.explain: Amino acids use specific transporters (e.g., neutral, acidic, basic amino acid transporters). Dipeptides and tripeptides use PepT1 (proton-coupled cotransporter). Both are active transport mechanisms.

questions.q: Why is the terminal ileum particularly important for nutrient absorption despite being only 1-2 meters long?
options: It has more villi than other regions
It is the site of vitamin B12 absorption and terminal iron absorption
It secretes most of the digestive enzymes
It is where most carbohydrate digestion occurs
questions.explain: The terminal ileum is the specialized site for vitamin B12 (via intrinsic factor receptor) and iron absorption. It's also where remaining nutrients are absorbed before reaching the large intestine.

questions.q: An exam trap about intestinal absorption: Which statement is INCORRECT?
options: Monosaccharides are absorbed by active transport
Fat-soluble vitamins require bile salts for absorption
Water absorption in the large intestine depends exclusively on osmotic gradients from electrolyte absorption
Short-chain fatty acids from bacterial fermentation are absorbed by colonocytes
questions.explain: Exam trap: While electrolyte absorption drives water absorption, tight junctions and aquaporins (water channels) are also essential. Water can't be absorbed if the epithelial barrier is damaged.

questions.q: Which absorption process would be most severely impaired if the intestinal epithelium were damaged (e.g., in celiac disease or inflammatory bowel disease)?
options: Absorption of ALL nutrients due to reduced surface area and transport capacity
questions.explain: Damage to the intestinal epithelium (flattened villi, reduced surface area, damaged transporters) impairs absorption of all nutrients: proteins, carbohydrates, fats, vitamins, minerals, and water.

questions.q: What is the functional significance of the mucus layer coating the large intestine?
options: Protects the epithelium from bacterial acid and abrasive fecal matter
questions.explain: Mucus (produced by goblet cells) creates a lubricant layer that protects colonocytes from bacteria, acid produced by fermentation, and mechanical trauma from fecal contents.

questions.q: How does the movement of sodium ions from the intestinal lumen into enterocytes create a driving force for other nutrient absorption?
options: Sodium directly binds to glucose transporters
Sodium creates an electrochemical gradient that drives secondary active transport of glucose and amino acids
questions.explain: Sodium's movement down its gradient (via Na/K-ATPase on the basolateral side) energizes secondary active transporters (SGLT1 for glucose, PepT1 for peptides) that couple nutrient uptake to sodium co-transport.

questions.q: Which organ system is primarily responsible for transporting absorbed lipids (chylomicrons) away from the small intestine?
questions.explain: Chylomicrons are too large to enter blood capillaries; they enter lacteals (lymphatic capillaries) and travel via the lymphatic system to the bloodstream, bypassing first-pass hepatic processing.

questions.q: What is an important difference between the absorption of short-chain carbohydrates (glucose) versus long-chain fatty acids in terms of transport into the blood?
options: Glucose enters the portal blood; fatty acids enter the lymphatic system as chylomicrons
Both enter the portal blood immediately
Both enter the lymphatic system initially
Glucose is absorbed by the large intestine; fatty acids by the small intestine
questions.explain: Water-soluble nutrients (glucose, amino acids) go directly into portal blood → liver. Lipids go into lymphatic system as chylomicrons, eventually reaching the bloodstream and tissues before the liver.

questions.q: In the large intestine, which microbial metabolite serves as a preferred fuel source for colonocytes?
questions.explain: Butyrate is produced by bacterial fermentation and is the preferred fuel for colonocytes. It provides ~10% of the body's energy. This is why dietary fiber and healthy microbiota are important.

questions.q: Which of the following would cause decreased absorption in the small intestine? (Select the MOST comprehensive answer)
questions.explain: All three factors are critical: bile for fat absorption, enzymes for nutrient breakdown, and epithelial integrity for transport. Loss of any one impairs absorption; combined loss is catastrophic.

questions.q: Why are villi and microvilli essential for nutrient absorption despite taking up cellular energy?
options: They increase surface area for transport, allowing adequate absorption despite short transit time (3-5 hours)
questions.explain: The small intestine transit time is only 3-5 hours. The massive surface area increase (villi + microvilli = 600-fold) allows complete nutrient extraction before food moves to the colon.

questions.q: How is iron absorption uniquely regulated in the small intestine?
options: Iron is actively transported regardless of body iron status
Iron absorption is regulated by hepcidin, which responds to body iron stores
The large intestine absorbs excess iron to prevent overload
Iron is always excreted in bile
questions.explain: Hepcidin (produced by the liver) regulates intestinal iron absorption. When iron stores are high, hepcidin blocks iron absorption by internalization of ferroportin. This prevents iron overload.

questions.q: Which region of the small intestine has the highest concentration of Peyer's patches?
questions.explain: The terminal ileum has the highest density of Peyer's patches (lymphoid tissue). These sample intestinal antigens and mount immune responses, protecting against pathogens while allowing nutrient absorption.

questions.q: How do intestinal secretions (mucus, water, electrolytes) contribute to digestion and absorption?
options: They dilute nutrients and reduce absorption efficiency
They create the optimal pH and osmotic environment for enzyme action and nutrient dissolution
They are reabsorbed entirely by the large intestine with no net loss
questions.explain: Intestinal secretions (2 L/day) provide water for nutrient dissolution, electrolytes for osmotic balance, and mucus for lubrication. Most water is reabsorbed, but secretions are essential for absorption.

questions.q: What is the relationship between intestinal motility and nutrient absorption?
options: Faster motility increases absorption by exposing more intestinal surface
Slower motility decreases absorption due to reduced time in intestine
Motility allows food to mix with digestive juices and maintain contact with absorptive epithelium
Motility has no direct relationship to absorption efficiency
questions.explain: Segmentation and peristalsis mix chyme with digestive juices and move it along slowly enough for absorption (3-5 hours transit time). Too-fast or too-slow motility impairs absorption.

questions.q: Why does the large intestine primarily absorb water and electrolytes rather than nutrients?
options: Nutrients have already been absorbed in the small intestine
Large intestine epithelial cells lack nutrient transporters
Water and electrolytes are more important for survival than nutrients
questions.explain: By the time chyme reaches the large intestine, ~90% of carbs, proteins, and fats have been absorbed. The large intestine's role is to salvage remaining water and electrolytes, and reabsorb short-chain fatty acids from fermentation.

lectures.title: Lecture 9: Neuroscience I — Neurons, Glia & Membrane Potentials

questions.q: Ion channels are distinguished from other membrane transport proteins by:
options: They have very high ion flux rates (>10^6 ions/sec) and operate passively
They actively pump ions against their gradient
They are found only in neurons
questions.explain: Ion channels are passive, high-flux devices (~10^6 ions/sec); they do not require ATP. This distinguishes them from active pumps and slower facilitated transporters.

questions.q: Which of the following is an example of PASSIVE transport?
options: Na+ being pumped out by Na+/K+ ATPase
K+ flowing through K+ leak channels
Ca2+ being pumped into the sarcoplasmic reticulum
questions.explain: K+ leak through channels is passive (down the gradient, no ATP). The others: Na+/K+ ATPase is active; SGLT1 is secondary active (Na+ gradient); Ca2+ pump is active.

questions.q: The Na+/K+ ATPase pumps:
options: Na+ and K+ in equal amounts
questions.explain: The Na+/K+ ATPase actively pumps 3 Na+ out and 2 K+ in per ATP molecule, creating and maintaining the ion gradients that establish the resting potential.

questions.q: What is the approximate resting membrane potential in a typical neuron?
questions.explain: The resting potential is ~–70 mV (inside negative). This is near the K+ equilibrium potential (~–90 mV) but slightly less negative due to some Na+ permeability.

questions.q: The K+ equilibrium potential is ~–90 mV. If a neuron's resting potential is –70 mV (less negative), this indicates:
options: The neuron is at K+ equilibrium
The membrane is more permeable to Na+ than K+ at rest
The Na+/K+ ATPase has stopped working
questions.explain: If resting potential (–70 mV) is less negative than K+ equilibrium (–90 mV), it means Na+ (equilibrium ~+60 mV) is contributing—the membrane is slightly permeable to Na+.

questions.q: The Nernst equation calculates:
options: The equilibrium potential for a single ion
The rate of ion channel opening
The ATP consumption by Na+/K+ ATPase
questions.explain: The Nernst equation gives the voltage at which diffusion and electrical forces balance for one ion (equilibrium potential). The resting potential is a weighted average of all ion equilibria.

questions.q: During the depolarization phase of an action potential:
options: Voltage-gated K+ channels open; K+ efflux repolarizes the cell
Voltage-gated Na+ channels open; Na+ influx makes the membrane potential more positive
Hyperpolarization begins due to K+ leaving
The membrane potential reaches its most negative value
questions.explain: Depolarization is caused by Na+ influx opening voltage-gated Na+ channels, driving the potential toward the Na+ equilibrium (~+60 mV), often overshooting to ~+30 mV.

questions.q: The all-or-nothing principle states that:
options: All neurons fire action potentials simultaneously
Once threshold is reached, an action potential proceeds to completion
Smaller stimuli produce smaller action potentials
The nervous system operates only if all conditions are met
questions.explain: Once threshold is reached, an action potential proceeds fully regardless of stimulus strength. Stronger stimuli do not produce larger potentials; they produce more frequent ones.

questions.q: Sodium channel inactivation is distinct from deactivation (closure) because:
options: Inactivated channels cannot open again immediately; this creates the refractory period
Inactivation only occurs in sensory neurons
Deactivation allows the channel to reopen immediately
questions.explain: Inactivation is a process where the channel is closed but also resistant to reopening until repolarization occurs; this creates the refractory period preventing immediate re-firing.

questions.q: During repolarization of an action potential:
options: Na+ channels open and depolarize the cell
Na+ channels inactivate; K+ channels open; K+ efflux repolarizes the cell
The membrane potential stabilizes at resting level
questions.explain: Repolarization: Na+ channels inactivate (blocking further Na+ entry), voltage-gated K+ channels open (delayed), K+ flows out, restoring negative potential.

questions.q: The refractory period exists because:
options: Na+ channels are inactivated and cannot reopen immediately
questions.explain: During the refractory period, Na+ channels are inactivated (a state distinct from simple closure), preventing another action potential from being initiated until channels recover.

questions.q: Continuous conduction (as in unmyelinated axons) is slower than saltatory conduction because:
options: Unmyelinated axons have fewer ion channels
Each region of the entire axon must depolarize to threshold sequentially
questions.explain: Continuous conduction requires the entire membrane to regenerate the action potential at each step (~0.5–2 m/s). Saltatory conduction skips myelinated regions (~1–100 m/s).

questions.q: The Nodes of Ranvier are:
options: Gaps in the myelin sheath where ion channels are concentrated
questions.explain: Nodes are unmyelinated gaps between myelin segments where Na+ and K+ channels cluster, enabling saltatory conduction (jumping between nodes).

questions.q: Saltatory conduction is more energy-efficient than continuous conduction because:
options: Fewer regions need to regenerate the action potential, so fewer ions must be pumped back by Na+/K+ ATPase
Saltatory axons do not fire action potentials
questions.explain: Saltatory conduction depolarizes only at nodes; continuous conduction depolarizes the entire membrane. Less ion flow means less work for the Na+/K+ ATPase to restore gradients.

questions.q: In the PNS, myelin is produced by:
questions.explain: In the PNS, Schwann cells produce myelin. One Schwann cell wraps one axon segment. In the CNS, oligodendrocytes produce myelin (one cell wraps multiple axons).

questions.q: A single oligodendrocyte (CNS) differs from a single Schwann cell (PNS) in that it:
options: Cannot conduct action potentials as quickly
questions.explain: One oligodendrocyte myelinates multiple axon segments; one Schwann cell myelinates only one axon segment. This is an important anatomical distinction.

questions.q: Which of the following is primarily responsible for establishing the K+/Na+ gradient at rest?
questions.explain: The Na+/K+ ATPase actively pumps ions to establish and maintain the gradient (3 Na+ out, 2 K+ in per ATP). Leak channels allow diffusion but do not establish gradients.

questions.q: Exam-style: If Na+/K+ ATPase function were blocked:
options: The resting potential would become less negative (depolarize)
Na+ would accumulate inside; K+ would accumulate outside
The ionic gradients would gradually dissipate
questions.explain: With the pump blocked, Na+ accumulates inside, K+ outside, gradients dissipate, and the resting potential depolarizes toward 0 mV over time.

questions.q: Which of the following best explains why myelin increases conduction velocity?
options: Myelin concentrates ion channels at the nodes, requiring fewer sites to regenerate the action potential
questions.explain: Myelin insulates the axon, confining ion channels to nodes. Only nodes need to depolarize to threshold, allowing the action potential to "jump" between them, greatly speeding conduction.

questions.q: The hyperpolarization (afterhyperpolarization) phase of an action potential occurs because:
options: Na+ channels remain open too long
K+ channels remain open slightly too long, driving potential below resting level
The stimulus intensity is too high
questions.explain: K+ channels close slightly slower than needed to return to resting potential, causing a brief overshoot below resting level (hyperpolarization) before stabilization.

questions.q: In an unmyelinated axon, local current flows:
options: Only inside the axon along the cytoplasm
Both inside and across the membrane, depolarizing adjacent regions
questions.explain: Local current flows inside the axon (longitudinal resistance) and across the membrane (transmembrane current), depolarizing adjacent regions to threshold in continuous conduction.

questions.q: Which ion equilibrium potential is closest to the resting membrane potential?
questions.explain: The resting potential (~–70 mV) is closest to K+ equilibrium (~–90 mV) because the membrane is most permeable to K+ at rest, though Na+ permeability shifts it slightly less negative.

questions.q: Which of the following correctly describes the relationship between stimulus strength and action potential frequency?
options: Stronger stimuli produce larger action potentials
Stronger stimuli produce action potentials with faster rising phases
Stronger stimuli produce more frequent action potentials (higher firing rate)
Stimulus strength does not affect action potential properties
questions.explain: The all-or-nothing principle: action potential size is constant. Stronger stimuli trigger more frequent firing (higher frequency), not larger potentials.

questions.q: Exam-style: If a neuron's membrane became more permeable to Na+ at rest:
options: The resting potential would become more positive (depolarize)
The resting potential would become more negative
The Na+ equilibrium potential would become more negative
questions.explain: The resting potential is a weighted average of ion equilibria. Increased Na+ permeability shifts the potential toward Na+ equilibrium (~+60 mV), causing depolarization.

questions.q: Which of the following is an example of a passive transporter (carrier)?
questions.explain: GLUT1 is facilitated diffusion (passive); others are active or channels.

questions.q: The fact that the resting potential (~–70 mV) is less negative than the K+ equilibrium potential (~–90 mV) indicates:
options: The cell has lost K+ to the outside
Na+ permeability contributes to the resting potential
questions.explain: Less negative potential means Na+ inward current is contributing.

questions.q: During an action potential, the membrane potential reaches a peak of ~+30 mV (overshoot) rather than stopping at the Na+ equilibrium potential (~+60 mV) because:
options: Repolarization begins before Na+ equilibrium is reached
K+ channels open before Na+ channels fully open
The stimulus drives the potential past equilibrium
Sodium channels are inactivated before equilibrium
questions.explain: K+ channels open as Na+ channels inactivate, beginning repolarization before equilibrium.

questions.q: Which of the following would cause the absolute refractory period to be longer?
questions.explain: Slower recovery from inactivation = longer refractory period.

questions.q: In saltatory conduction, the distance between nodes of Ranvier affects:
options: The diameter of the axon only
questions.explain: Longer internodal distances = faster conduction.

questions.q: Astrocytes in the CNS primarily function to:
options: Provide metabolic support and regulate extracellular ion concentrations
questions.explain: Astrocytes provide metabolic support and buffer extracellular K+.

lectures.title: Lecture 10: Neuroscience II — Synaptic Transmission & Neural Circuits

questions.q: A sensory receptor is best defined as:
options: Any nerve ending in the skin
A specialized cell that detects a stimulus and converts it into an electrical signal
A region of the spinal cord
questions.explain: A sensory receptor detects a specific stimulus (via a receptor protein) and transduces it into an electrical signal (receptor potential) the nervous system can process.

questions.q: Which of the following is an example of a mechanoreceptor?
options: Rod or cone in the retina
questions.explain: Hair cells (mechanoreceptors in the inner ear) detect sound and acceleration. Rods/cones are photoreceptors; taste buds are chemoreceptors; olfactory cells detect chemicals.

questions.q: Sensory transduction involves:
options: Amplification of a weak stimulus into an action potential
Conversion of stimulus energy into a receptor potential
Transmission of information directly to the cerebral cortex
questions.explain: Transduction is the process of converting a stimulus (energy) into an electrical signal (receptor potential). This is the first step in sensory processing.

questions.q: Adaptation in sensory receptors means that:
options: The receptor becomes sensitive to other stimuli
Prolonged stimulation causes a decreased receptor response
The receptor loses its ability to function
questions.explain: Adaptation is a reduction in receptor response to sustained stimuli—the receptor produces a smaller signal even though the stimulus continues, allowing detection of changes.

questions.q: A sensory receptor adapts because:
options: The nervous system stops paying attention
Changes in intracellular signaling cascade (e.g., phosphorylation of ion channels) reduce the response to sustained stimuli
questions.explain: Adaptation is a molecular property of the receptor itself—sustained stimulation triggers negative feedback (phosphorylation, desensitization, channel closure) that reduces the response.

questions.q: Which of the following correctly describes the order of neurons in a sensory pathway?
options: First-order: periphery to spinal cord; second-order: spinal cord to thalamus; third-order: thalamus to cortex
First-order: cortex to thalamus; second-order: thalamus to spinal cord; third-order: spinal cord to periphery
The order varies by sensory modality with no consistent pattern
All neurons are equivalent in sensory pathways
questions.explain: The canonical pathway: first-order (sensory receptors/axons), second-order (spinal cord/brainstem), third-order (thalamus) → cortex.

questions.q: An exception to the typical three-neuron sensory pathway is:
options: Vision (via optic nerve to lateral geniculate nucleus)
Smell (olfactory receptors send directly to the olfactory cortex, bypassing the thalamus)
questions.explain: Olfaction is unique: olfactory receptors (first-order) synapse directly in the olfactory bulb, which projects to olfactory cortex, bypassing the thalamus.

questions.q: Rate coding in sensory systems refers to:
options: The speed of action potentials along an axon
The coding of stimulus intensity by the frequency of action potentials
The temporal pattern of synaptic transmission
The number of neurons responding to a stimulus
questions.explain: Rate coding: stimulus intensity is encoded by action potential frequency. A stronger stimulus produces a larger receptor potential, triggering action potentials at higher frequency.

questions.q: Temporal coding is particularly important in:
options: Vision, where color is encoded by firing rate
Touch, where pressure is encoded by firing rate
Audition, where sound location is encoded by the precise timing of action potentials in auditory neurons
Taste, where bitter is encoded by timing
questions.explain: Phase locking in auditory neurons uses timing to encode sound location—neurons fire in synchrony with specific phases of sound waves, providing timing information.

questions.q: A sensory neuron cannot increase its firing rate indefinitely because of:
options: The refractory period limiting how frequently action potentials can fire
Depletion of neurotransmitter at the sensory ending
Loss of sensitivity due to adaptation
questions.explain: The absolute refractory period sets a limit on maximum firing frequency (~500–1000 Hz max, but typically lower). Adaptation and transmitter limits also exist but are secondary.

questions.q: A receptive field is:
options: The region of a receptor protein where stimuli bind
The region of sensory space that activates a neuron
The synapse between a sensory receptor and a second-order neuron
The threshold voltage for action potential generation
questions.explain: A receptive field is the spatial or sensory region (skin area, visual field, etc.) to which a neuron responds. It has an excitatory center and often an inhibitory surround.

questions.q: Center-surround receptive field organization means:
options: The receptive field is always circular
Stimulation in the center excites the neuron; stimulation in the surround inhibits it
The receptive field is organized by the cortex
questions.explain: Center-surround: central excitatory region (stimulus increases firing) surrounded by inhibitory region (stimulus decreases firing). This sharpens contrast at stimulus edges.

questions.q: Lateral inhibition enhances perception by:
options: Sharpening contrast at the edges of a stimulus
Increasing the overall sensitivity of all neurons
questions.explain: Lateral inhibition suppresses responses at the edges of a stimulus (via nearby inhibitory neurons), sharpening the perceptual contrast and edge definition.

questions.q: The phenomenon of Mach bands (illusory bright and dark bands near stimulus edges) is caused by:
options: Lateral inhibition creating artificial contrast enhancement
Increased firing of neurons at the stimulus boundary
questions.explain: Mach bands are an illusion where lateral inhibition creates artificial dark bands on the dark side of a boundary and bright bands on the light side, demonstrating neural processing distorts perception.

questions.q: When a sensory neuron's action potential reaches the axon terminal:
options: The action potential directly opens channels in the postsynaptic cell
Voltage-gated Ca2+ channels open, triggering vesicle release of neurotransmitter
The postsynaptic cell fires an action potential immediately
questions.explain: Ca2+ influx triggers synaptic vesicle fusion and neurotransmitter release into the synaptic cleft, where it binds postsynaptic receptors (chemical transmission, not electrical).

questions.q: Excitatory synapses in sensory systems typically use:
questions.explain: Glutamate is the primary excitatory neurotransmitter in the nervous system. GABA and glycine are inhibitory.

questions.q: Inhibitory synapses typically use:
questions.explain: GABA (CNS, 脊髓) and glycine (spinal cord, 脊髓) are the primary inhibitory neurotransmitters.

questions.q: A major advantage of chemical synaptic transmission over electrical transmission is:
options: Chemical transmission allows signal amplification and processing (summation, inhibition)
questions.explain: Chemical transmission is slower (~1 ms delay) but allows filtering (inhibition can override excitation), summation (temporal and spatial), and complex signal processing before the postsynaptic cell responds.

questions.q: Temporal summation refers to:
options: Multiple neurons firing at different times
Multiple inputs arriving at the postsynaptic cell at different times, with overlapping effects
The timing of action potential propagation along an axon
The duration of the refractory period
questions.explain: Temporal summation: multiple EPSCs (excitatory postsynaptic currents) arriving within a short window summate and can reach threshold if the postsynaptic potential reaches threshold.

questions.q: Spatial summation refers to:
options: The size of the receptive field
Multiple synaptic inputs from different locations on the postsynaptic cell that summate to produce a larger response
The distance action potentials travel along an axon
The spread of neurotransmitter in the synaptic cleft
questions.explain: Spatial summation: EPSCs from multiple synapses (different regions of the cell) summate. If total depolarization reaches threshold, an action potential fires.

questions.q: Exam-style: How does a sensory system detect a very weak stimulus despite neuronal noise?
options: Sensory receptors amplify weak signals before synaptic transmission
Multiple sensory neurons converge on the same postsynaptic neuron, allowing temporal and spatial summation of weak inputs
Weak stimuli activate only a few neurons, which is sufficient
questions.explain: Weak stimuli activate multiple sensory neurons with overlapping receptive fields; their summed input at a postsynaptic cell can reach threshold, allowing detection of weak signals.

questions.q: If a sensory receptor's G-protein signaling cascade is blocked:
options: The receptor potential would be reduced or absent
The stimulus would be perceived more intensely
questions.explain: Many receptors use G-protein cascades (→ second messengers → ion channels). Blocking this cascade prevents ion channel opening and receptor potential generation.

questions.q: Exam-style: In the dorsal column-medial lemniscal pathway (proprioception and touch):
options: First-order neurons bypass the thalamus and project directly to cortex
First-order neurons ascend the spinal cord in the dorsal columns; second-order neurons are in the medulla
Second-order neurons project directly to the spinal cord from the cortex
All three orders of neurons are in the spinal cord
questions.explain: First-order: peripheral receptor → dorsal column (ascending ipsilaterally); second-order: medial lemniscus (crossing in medulla) → thalamus; third-order: thalamus → cortex.

questions.q: Which of the following is a G-protein–coupled receptor mechanism?
options: Direct ion channel opening by neurotransmitter
Receptor protein → G protein → adenylyl cyclase → cAMP → ion channel opening
Receptor protein forms a channel directly
Neurotransmitter opening a ligand-gated ion channel
questions.explain: G-protein coupling uses second messengers (cAMP, IP3).

questions.q: Why would a sensory system with a small receptive field be better at detecting small, localized stimuli?
options: Smaller receptive fields are more sensitive
Localization of the stimulus is more precise with small receptive fields
Small receptive fields do not require adaptation
Small receptive fields respond to all stimuli equally
questions.explain: Small receptive fields provide better spatial resolution.

questions.q: A patient with damage to the dorsal root ganglion would lose:
options: All sensation from the body region supplied by that ganglion
questions.explain: First-order neurons in the dorsal root ganglion are the primary sensory pathway.

questions.q: Synaptic plasticity allows sensory systems to:
options: Learn which stimuli are important and which are irrelevant
questions.explain: LTP and LTD allow learning: frequently used synapses strengthen.

questions.q: If all inhibitory synapses in a sensory system were blocked:
options: The system would be more sensitive to all stimuli
Lateral inhibition would be lost, reducing contrast sharpening
Only excitatory synapses would remain functional
questions.explain: Loss of lateral inhibition reduces contrast sharpening.

questions.q: Exam-style: A person is exposed to a constant odor. Why do they stop noticing it after a few minutes even though the odor is still present?
options: Olfactory receptor adaptation reduces the receptor potential to sustained stimuli
The brain chooses to ignore the odor
questions.explain: Olfactory receptor adaptation reduces response to sustained odors.

questions.q: Why is the ~1 ms synaptic delay (chemical transmission) beneficial compared to faster electrical transmission?
options: Chemical transmission allows inhibition to override excitation
Chemical transmission allows temporal and spatial summation
Chemical transmission allows neural processing and computation
questions.explain: Chemical transmission's delay enables filtering, summation, and processing.
